name: functional-patterns
description: Functional programming patterns that promote testability, composability, and maintainability.
capabilities: Create pure functions that are deterministic and side-effect free
Implement immutable state updates using spread operators and array methods
Compose functions using pipe and compose patterns for readable data flows
Handle errors gracefully with the Result pattern instead of exceptions
Apply dependency injection via function parameters for testable code
Use Option/Maybe pattern to safely handle nullable values
author: DoubleslashSE
securitySummary: This skill is pure documentation containing educational TypeScript code examples. The static scanner flagged 36 false positives due to pattern-matching limitations: SHA-256 hashes in JSON metadata were misidentified as weak crypto/C2 indicators, and backticks in TypeScript template literals were misidentified as shell execution. This is a documentation-only skill with no executable code, scripts, network calls, filesystem access, or external command execution capabilities.
valueStatement: Node.js code often suffers from side effects and implicit dependencies that make testing difficult and bugs hard to trace. This skill provides proven functional patterns including pure functions, immutable updates, and monadic error handling to write maintainable TypeScript code.
limitations: Pattern examples are in TypeScript and may require adaptation for plain JavaScript
Does not include runtime libraries or executable code
Assumes basic familiarity with TypeScript types and functions

useCases.title: Build Testable APIs
useCases.description: Create API handlers with pure business logic and injected dependencies for reliable unit tests

useCases.title: Standardize Code Patterns
useCases.description: Establish consistent error handling and state management across a shared codebase

useCases.title: Validate Code Quality
useCases.description: Review pull requests against functional principles for maintainability and testability

promptTemplates.title: Pure Function Basics
promptTemplates.scenario: Converting an impure function to pure
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to convert this impure function to a pure function. The function calculates order totals and saves to database.

promptTemplates.title: Immutable Updates
promptTemplates.prompt: Write an immutable update function that changes a deeply nested property in a user object without mutating the original.

promptTemplates.title: Function Composition
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a pipe function that chains trim, validate, and transform operations on user input strings.

promptTemplates.title: Result Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Convert this function that uses try-catch blocks to use the Result pattern with map and flatMap operations.

bestPractices: Keep business logic pure by separating it from I/O operations like database calls and network requests
Use immutable updates with spread operators and array methods rather than mutating objects in place
Return Result types from functions instead of throwing exceptions to make error handling explicit
antiPatterns: Mixing I/O with business logic in the same function destroys testability
Using mutable state and shared variables creates hidden dependencies
Throwing exceptions for control flow hides error conditions from type signatures

faq.question: Is this skill compatible with plain JavaScript projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The patterns work in JavaScript but TypeScript provides better type safety for Result and Option patterns.

faq.question: What is the performance impact of immutable updates?
faq.answer: Modern JavaScript engines optimize spread operations well. For most applications the overhead is negligible compared to correctness benefits.

faq.question: How does this integrate with existing codebases?
faq.answer: Apply patterns incrementally. Start by isolating new code with pure functions, then gradually refactor existing code.

faq.question: Does this skill store or transmit any user data?
faq.answer: No. This is a pure documentation skill with no code execution, network access, or file system operations.

faq.question: Why use Result instead of exceptions?
faq.answer: Result types make errors explicit in the type signature, force handling at compile time, and compose cleanly with functional operations.

faq.question: How does this compare to object-oriented patterns?
faq.answer: Functional patterns emphasize immutable data and pure functions. OOP emphasizes encapsulated state and behavior. Both can coexist in projects.
